items.title: Area: Lake District
items.cleanTitle: Lake District
items.description:
The newest sub area of the larger Cave Fork Recreational Preserve (CFRP). The Lake District offers a nice concentration of crags surrounding the lake. These new crags offer styles of climbing for everyone, so come explore and test the waters. 

This area is still in development, this is just a placeholder until trails and belay infrastructure are put in.

Park in the designated RRGCC parking lot located here: https://maps.app.goo.gl/Hcd2Ds6TsBct4zfF9 

The gravel road around the lake is private property, climbers have permission to walk on this road BUT NOT TO DRIVE, even if the gate is open, your car may be locked in or towed. Also, do not swim, fish, or boat in the lake.

All clifftop properties are a part of the Highlands development; these newly sold lots will have cabin construction near clifftops.  Please be vigilant, and if you see any evidence of debris being thrown over the cliff please report to the RRGCC. 


Kentucky > Red River Gorge > (CFRP) Cave Fork Recr…
Shared By: Haskie D

items.title: Route: Adore You (5.13a)
items.cleanTitle: Adore You (5.13a)
items.description:
Climb on 5 degree until the first ledge after bolt 3. Then continue on 45 degrees until the anchors. Crux in between bolt 6-7. Belay on First ascent by Tod Mansfield. Project belays : Audrey Sniezek Camille Avestruz, Ephraim VanFleet and Matt Becker. Photos by Nate Foster, Alex Eddlemon, Alex Kalman and Hailing Bao.

items.title: Route: The Devil Wears Prana (5.12a)
items.cleanTitle: The Devil Wears Prana (5.12a)
items.description:
Start up the slab doing some sequential moves through slots and pockets. Some moderate face climbing leads to a seemingly section of wall navigating through some "bubbles" on the wall. When you get to the handle bar hold and the wall kicks back, switch gears and make a big move on crimps to launch into pumpy climbing to the chains.

items.title: Comment re: Eureka: An all-time climb—you’ve got to do it. Beginne...
items.cleanTitle: An all-time climb—you’ve got to do it. Beginne...
items.description:
An all-time climb—you’ve got to do it. Beginner leader caution: the bolts are a bit spaced out.



Due to the new cabins/rental situation you may be forced to listen to someone else’s party music radio and their impressively loud chatter as you enjoy this world-class adventure in a woods that would otherwise be full of birdsong and rustling leaves. The situation reminds me we must protect not only access but environment and atmosphere too.



As for the environment, the approach trail straight to Eureka (that doesn’t actually have rebar) is tearing up all the dirt under a tree’s root system. An alternative route up the final hill or a set of wooden stairs would do wonders for the erosion.
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > Natural Bridge… > Global Village > Eureka (5.6)
Shared By: Tomo Dugan

items.title: Comment re: Foxfire: Some of the best adventure climbing in RRG imo. We...
items.cleanTitle: Some of the best adventure climbing in RRG imo. We...
items.description:
Some of the best adventure climbing in RRG imo. We did this route in three pitches. My thoughts are below.



The approach: Park at the Copperas Falls Trailhead (arrive early). From the lot, keep right toward the Red River and follow the unofficial riverside path. The route description describes the approach well.



Pitch 1: This pitch will feel a lot better if you have a #5 or a #6 to protect down low. Also be mindful that using the tree to layback on and shimmy up is difficult with a pack on.



Pitch 2: Break down the anchor and walk right unroped along a wide ledge. Follow it past the thin orange cracks to a bigger, crumbly-looking crack and set up a belay. There’s some loose rock low on this pitch, so watch it. Climb the crack to a very small ledge. It will be tempting to follow the chalked face climbing and head right onto the face. Bad idea. Stay LEFT in the crack and keep going up.



You’ll hit another ledge with a small tree way off to the right, this is not your army crawl (ask me how I know). From here, look up to a little ledge about 8 feet up with a small roof. At your feet, a horizontal crack takes a #2 (beware of rock quality), this is likely your last gear before the anchor and probably why this pitch gets the R rating. Top out left onto ledge, and you’ll finally reach the army crawl and tree everyone talks about. The tree is a little on the small size for natural pro and your follower is at a significant fall risk coming around the army crawl. Next time we do this I am going to try and build an anchor in the rock at the back of the ledge. Which sets you up perfect for Pitch 3.



Pitch 3: First off, rope drag is going to be a bitch, and following isn’t much better. Work your way up the wall, up the crack on the right and to the ledge with the roof.



From the ledge, we went far left and followed the crack. Not sure if that’s the intended line, but it felt stiff for 5.7 with pretty sparse pro. My partner got a black Tricam in, then basically ran it out to the top. There are two trees at the top where you can build a belay from (beware copperheads here! ). If your follower comes off route, a 3:1 could really help haul them through the cruxy bits with all that drag. I loved the first two pitches but this one, not so much.



Descent: We tried to rap. Mistake. Our 70m was maxed on both raps and was a great reminder of why you always close the system. We rapped top to the Pitch 2 ledge, then from a big tree down toward the Pitch 1 ledge and we still had to thread through trees to reach the ground. Honestly, we could’ve just walked off and been back at the car way faster. Still, a fun day and a beautiful view from the top.
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > Northern Gorge > Eagle Point But… > Foxfire (5.7 R)
Shared By: Hannah Wright

items.title: Comment re: The Dread Pirate Roberts: My take? This thing freaking rocks! 10/10. So far,...
items.cleanTitle: My take? This thing freaking rocks! 10/10. So far,...
items.description:
My take? This thing freaking rocks! 10/10. So far, this is my favorite route that I have climbed in CRP (which is not many). But this was the most fun route I have climbed in a good while. It's a great length, super fun moves, well protected, and did I mention super fun moves?



Now, between the first and fourth bolt is what I think is the crux. If you try to climb in the super flared crack to go over the bulge, more power to you. It's so flared that it's so hard to get a good jam, and I think it would push this climb beyond 5.9 moves.



I think the intended beta is to climb left at the bulge and then traverse right after you clip the fourth bolt, or when you run out of good hands.

items.title: Comment re: Sandbagger: So! My partner and I were talking about this route...
items.cleanTitle: So! My partner and I were talking about this route...
items.description:
So! My partner and I were talking about this route for a good while after climbing it. Here are some thoughts:



1. It's a fun route, plain and simple! Big moves, awesome line. The moves are not technical, they are just big, pumpy, and powerful.

2. The Yosemite Decimal System is very subjective, and so many factors play into it. It also doesn't account for differences in body proportions among climbers. I am 5'8", and I found the start doable, but I can imagine some shorter climbers really struggling with the start of the route.

3. I do think this is a 5.9+. The moves are very 5.9+, they are not that technical. However, if you are not the strongest climber at sustained and slightly overhung climbing and just don't have the power, you will probably struggle (like I did!). My buddy, who is taller than me, felt that it fell more into the 5.10b category. So, just remember,it's all subjective, and climb what's fun for you!
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > (CRP) Cliffview… > 19th Hole > Main Wall > Sandbagger (5.9+)

Definitely tougher than other 5.6 routes in the Red so I think that grade is a bit of a sandbag but it also isn't a 5.9. A good amount of jugs but not all that straightforward compared to other routes of the grade here, and the top is indeed harder for shorties like me. items.cleanTitle: A fun 5.7 that's true to grade and a bit easier th...
items.description:
A fun 5.7 that's true to grade and a bit easier than Bee's Business and Sweet Jane. Last move is a steep layback but on a huge flake so just haul up on it and clip the chains.



Safety note: When belaying folks heavier than me, I've been pulled towards the edge of the drop-off while lowering due to the weird angle (belayer stands to the left of the route which traverses right). I found it safer to either anchor lighter belayers to the lowest bolt or tie the end of the rope to the tree by the bench and clove to the belayer's harness.

items.title: Comment re: Pump Up the Jam: I cleaned the shit out of this thing. It's actuall...
items.cleanTitle: I cleaned the shit out of this thing. It's actuall...
items.description:
I cleaned the shit out of this thing. It's actually quite climbable now. The smears on the top and the jugs to finish are much more obvious and easier to use without so much mud and moss.



My beta was a right hand flaring hand jam with a .3/.2 nest in the roof crack and then crank really hard and tight all the way up to the left hand wide hand/fist jam. Pull through onto the banger jams and toss a .75 in there and finish it up.



Regarding difficulty I can't compare much in the grade at the red. Definitely harder than Riptide Ride and easier than Rebar so right there in the middle seems accurate but rebar is kind of a sandbag anyway.

items.description:
WOW! I agree with this being top 3 14a's in Kentucky. I no longer have a 6' ape so I could not make the span, but the move definitely still goes as a wild and commiting comp-style stand-up 'dyno', described as a 'lurp' above. I would suggest adding a bolt in the long run out, I've exploded the top biner of a quickdraw twice in my Climbing career on similar dynamic moves, and if that happens here you'll be in the dirt. 6/5 stars! Very low endurance requirement for the grade, but I guess the risk and complexity make up for it? RIC 5/2/4

items.title: Comment re: Bobsledding: This might be the hardest 5.7 I have ever climbed....
items.cleanTitle: This might be the hardest 5.7 I have ever climbed....
items.description:
This might be the hardest 5.7 I have ever climbed. Once you move past the roof it opens up into more legitimate 5.6-5.5 climbing and becomes very straightforward. In classic gorge fashion, the last ten feet is absolutely filthy and packed with dirt. Great climb!



For the roof, 0.3-0.5 BD cams seem to fit best in the first section.



The anchor is a mess of tat at various stages of decay, wrapped around a tree to the left of where the route ends. If you plan on climbing this, consider bringing some fresh rings and webbing
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > Northern Gorge > Phantasia > Bobsledding (5.7)
Shared By: Nolan Buland

items.title: Comment re: The Wasp: If you like spice, you've come to the right route....
items.cleanTitle: If you like spice, you've come to the right route....
items.description:
If you like spice, you've come to the right route. The lichen section is the mental and physical crux, but the rock is solid. Didn't break anything. The climbing is 10a but don't expect a chalked up tic-tac-toe experience; you will be searching for almost every hold.



Choose your own adventure for an anchor but I think the tree directly above the line is not rooted very well into the ground. It held my body weight but I could see the ground starting to pull up and some of the bottom of the tree is rotted- I opted instead to sling it and put cord for an anchor on an evergreen that was a bit further back and to the right. Adds some drag but is a better choice.
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > Northern Gorge > Fortress Wall > Wasp (5.10a PG13)
Shared By: Mike Wilkinson

items.title: Comment re: Unknown Chimney: To start, everyone's risk tolerance and risk asses...
items.cleanTitle: To start, everyone's risk tolerance and risk asses...
items.description:
To start, everyone's risk tolerance and risk assessment methods are highly individualized. People are going to weigh the dangers of any given climb differently, and that's completely valid.



With that said, this route is outside my risk tolerance. I made it about 15 feet up before deciding to bail. The rock was actively falling apart, with football-sized chunks breaking off with every move. This wasn't just a thin layer of surface sand the rock quality itself was genuinely terrible. Honestly some of the worst I've come across in the gorge. I can't say whether it's always been this way, but it felt completely unmanageable for me.
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > Northern Gorge > Phantasia > Unknown Chimney (5.8)
Shared By: Nolan Buland

items.title: Comment re: Tarr Ridge: Went up to Tarr Ridge today and had a couples comm...
items.cleanTitle: Went up to Tarr Ridge today and had a couples comm...
items.description:
Went up to Tarr Ridge today and had a couples comments and questions.



First on the approach— for anyone using the MP gps dot, it’ll take you right to the base of Prohibition.

The hike is steep and lots of bushwhacking. On our way down we saw a brand new trail camera. I was wondering if anyone had ever started hiking up the hill closer to the right pull off with the gate? Or any other beta on the approach besides “walk straight up the hill”?

We also found an awesome looking crack 100 ft left of prohibition with a bail cam about 15 feet up. The route looks like a stellar 75-90ft hand crack in the 5.9+ range with potential belay/rap tree to the right of the route. It does look incredibly sandy though. Any info on this route? Or the owner of the cam?

Another amazing looking crack up here we found on our hike over to “bush boy boogie”. Looked like a 40-50 ft roof crack approximately hand-sized. Anyone ever try this route? Looks incredible and hard from the ground. Happy to send pics and approx location to anyone interested.
Kentucky > Red River Gorge > Northern Gorge > Tarr Ridge
Shared By: Abbi Dingus-Simmons

items.title: Route: Code Red (5.13a)
items.cleanTitle: Code Red (5.13a)
items.description:
Can you crimp so hard you’ll give yourself a blood blister? You’re primed and ready to go for this nails hard face climb. Skin is a big factor as well as conditions, this climb gets morning shade and that’s when you want to be here. Trying to use the thin crimps guarding the first two bolts with sweaty tips is a fool’s errand.  

The crux is right off the ground, a lesson in technique and pain tolerance, these crimps are as sharp as they get in the Red and the feet are small. Once you gain the wave feature, a significantly more approachable boulder problem awaits, balance hard and fight to get to the well deserved no hands rest.

Get it together and tech your way to the top on the sparsely available holds hidden in the checkerboard head wall. The climbing is fun and all time, but don’t think you’re out of the weeds until clipping the final bolt.

The view from the top is incredible and the pitch is a true, hard-man’s face climb. Original grade is 12d, maybe some holds broke because this now feels every bit of 13a.



Kentucky > Red River Gorge > (PMRP) Pendergr… > Sore Heel Hollow > What About Bob Wall
Shared By: Nate Culberson

items.title: Comment re: UltraVibe Pleasure 2000: We climbed here last week and were surprised when ...
items.cleanTitle: We climbed here last week and were surprised when ...
items.description:
We climbed here last week and were surprised when a 4' snake slid past the group next to us and headed straight for the wall (we think it was a central rat snake). We were even more surprised when it started to climb the crack a few feet to the left of this route! We watched it make its way pretty high up to a larger opening about 5' left of the third bolt -- then we observed at least one other snake head poking out from that hole in the wall. Not sure if there's a nest there or not, but definitely keep an eye out if you're climbing either of the 10s in this part of the wall!
